content: The most pervasive and dangerous mistake beginners make when collecting errors in Rust is attempting to combine a collection of Results into a single Result without understanding the distinction between the naive approach and the idiomatic solution. When you have a Vec<Result<T>> representing a batch operation, such as reading multiple files or executing concurrent tasks, the naive instinct is often to use simple iteration logic that ignores context or fails prematurely on the first error encountered. This leads to two distinct anti-patterns: failing immediately upon encountering the first Error and losing all diagnostic information because only one failure was recorded, or attempting to unwrap every element blindly which causes a panic at runtime when the final result cannot be determined statically. The correct approach relies entirely on the Result combinator provided by the standard library in std::result::Result, specifically implementing either CollectMany (fail-first) or CombineAll (fail-last). When you need fail-fast behavior where any single failure invalidates the entire batch, you should use CollectMany which propagates the first encountered Error into a Vec<T> containing only that error alongside valid successes or simply returns an Err immediately depending on your specific logic. Conversely, if you require comprehensive reporting where every failed item must be documented regardless of other outcomes, you must implement CombineAll to aggregate all errors from each Result into a single vector of Errors while preserving the original structure of successful values. The performance implications are significant because naive manual iteration with conditional unwrapping or panic handling prevents the compiler from optimizing away runtime checks and forces explicit error propagation logic at every step rather than delegating to optimized built-in combinators. Furthermore, mixing failure modes creates subtle bugs where code paths diverge unexpectedly when an early exit occurs versus a full sweep completion. You must explicitly choose your strategy based on whether the application requires immediate feedback on any single point of failure or complete audit trails for batch processing operations. The idiomatic Rust solution avoids these pitfalls by leveraging traits like CollectMany and CombineAll which are designed specifically to handle these semantic differences cleanly without manual error handling code cluttering your logic.
